About Insights Free Tool The Audit
Private brief · Prepared for Flash Hansen · Updated May 2026 with seven-engine coverage

Five things AI engines can't see about Blue Note Hawaii. We track seven AI engines now. None of them can find the venue.

00 Why this email exists

Flash — Lance from Never Ranked. Carl Anderson and I are friends, and you and I crossed paths briefly a while back. He mentioned what you're building at Blue Note Hawaii on the marketing side, and I wanted to send you something specific instead of a generic introduction.

The short version. Most ticket-buying decisions for jazz, dinner-and-a-show, and Waikiki nightlife now run through an AI step before they hit your booking page. Someone in the lobby of the Outrigger, or someone planning their trip from the mainland, asks ChatGPT or Perplexity or their iPhone "where should I see live jazz in Honolulu," gets one or two recommendations, and books one. Blue Note Hawaii is either in those recommendations or not.

I ran the venue page through our public schema scorer this evening. The technical baseline is strong (an 82 out of 100, green zone — ahead of most venues we've audited). But there are five specific gaps that matter for the engines that surface "live jazz Honolulu tonight." Each one of them probably costs you tickets. Below is what I found.

00.5 The picture

Before the gap analysis, the visual. Eight identity signals AI engines lean on when surfacing live-music recommendations. Blue Note Hawaii lights some, misses others. The chart updates live each time this page loads.

01 What you have today

bluenotejazz.com/hawaii loads with five JSON-LD schema nodes and scores 82/100 on our completeness grader. For context, sites under 60 on this grader give AI engines materially fewer structured signals to cite. You are not in that zone. The site is technically clean. 

Detected schema       Grade
WebPage               95  green
ImageObject           95  green
BreadcrumbList       100  green
WebSite               95  green
Organization          75  gold

Organization is in the gold zone (deploys, but flagged for review) because it's missing five recommended fields that AI engines look at as identity anchors: address, contactPoint, founder, foundingDate, description. Filling those is straightforward. They aren't the headline issue.

02 The five things AI engines can't see

These are the gaps the schema audit surfaced. Each one maps to a specific class of customer query that you are probably losing.

Gap 01

No Event schema. Anywhere.

When someone asks Perplexity "what's playing at Blue Note Hawaii this weekend" or asks Siri "live jazz Waikiki tonight," the engines look for structured Event data. You don't publish any. The lineup is in your CMS but it isn't structured for AI to read. Which means the engines fall back to scraping or, more often, recommending a venue that does publish Event schema. Every show on your calendar should have a JSON-LD Event node with date, performer, ticket offer, and location. Adding this once and templating it for every show is a one-time engineering investment with compounding returns.

Gap 02

No LocalBusiness or NightClub schema.

You publish Organization schema, which AI engines treat as a brand entity, not a place to physically go. For "best jazz club in Honolulu" or "live music near Outrigger Waikiki," the engines need a venue-class schema (LocalBusiness or NightClub) with hours, geo coordinates, address, and amenities. Without it, the engines can't be confident enough to recommend you over a venue that has it. This is the highest-leverage single fix for ticket sales because it directly changes whether you appear in "places near me" type queries that drive walk-up traffic.

Gap 03

No AggregateRating schema.

When someone asks "is Blue Note Hawaii worth it" or "compare Blue Note Hawaii to other Honolulu jazz clubs," AI engines pull rating data from external sources you don't control (Yelp, Tripadvisor, Google). With AggregateRating schema published on your own site, the rating you stand behind becomes the canonical signal. This matters more than it sounds because the external sources frequently contradict each other and the engines split the difference, which usually hurts.

Gap 04

No Offer schema for tickets.

Cover charge, ticket price, package pricing — none of that is structured. When a customer asks "how much is Blue Note Hawaii" or compares your prices to alternatives, the engines either guess (badly) or skip you in favor of a venue that publishes prices in structured form. Offer schema attached to each Event also unlocks "free vs paid" filtering on some engines, which matters for tourist-research workflows.

Gap 05

Organization schema is incomplete.

Address, contactPoint, founder, foundingDate, description — all missing from the existing Organization node. None of these are show-stoppers individually. Together they make AI engines less confident in citing you as an authoritative source on yourself. The Blue Note Jazz Club has a 40-year history that the engines should be able to surface. Right now they can't pull it from your structured data.

03 Why this is happening now

Three things have shifted in the last 12 months that turn this from "nice to have" into "you are probably already losing tickets you can't see."

  • Apple Intelligence + Perplexity in Safari are now default-on for millions of iPhone users visiting Hawaii. Their first question to the AI is often a venue or activity question, and the AI answer is opaque — you can't see it in your analytics.
  • Google AI Overviews surface above the blue links for "live music Honolulu" type queries. Your Yelp listing might rank well, but if the AI Overview names a different venue, the click happens there.
  • ChatGPT now includes web-grounded search in its default responses. People are increasingly asking ChatGPT for trip recommendations and getting cited venue names. We track this directly.

None of these surfaces show up in your Google Analytics. They are real traffic sources that you cannot currently measure or influence. The schema gaps above are the levers that make Blue Note Hawaii appear in these answers.

04 Why NeverRanked, honestly

Most AEO tools tell you what to do. We do it. As of May 2026, NeverRanked is the only AEO platform we have evaluated that ships all of the following together:

  • Seven-engine citation tracking: Perplexity, ChatGPT, Gemini, Google AI Overviews (live via DataForSEO), Claude, Microsoft Copilot via Bing, and Gemma (Google's open-weight model, added May 2026). No other AEO platform tracks all seven. Profound and Athena cover three to four. Ahrefs Brand Radar covers four.
  • Done-for-you schema deployment via a one-line snippet. No CMS access, no dev cycle. Profound, Athena HQ, Ahrefs Brand Radar all stop at "here is what to deploy." We deploy it.
  • Multi-pass AI validation on every generated output. Factual grounding against the source, tone check, quality gate, three-attempt regeneration. Most "AI content" tools ship Claude's first draft. We refuse to.
  • Variant impact attribution: every schema we deploy is tracked as a variant. Citation rate measured 4 weeks before deploy vs 4 weeks after, two-proportion z-test, statistical confidence at p < 0.05. Your monthly Visibility Index includes a "what we deployed and what it did" section. Nobody else does this.
  • Prominence and sentiment within citations, not just yes/no. Where in the AI's answer were you mentioned (rank 1 hero quote vs rank 8 footnote)? Was the tone positive or negative? That's the next layer of AEO that most platforms have not built yet.
  • Real-time citation alerts on Signal+: gained or lost citations between monthly reports get emailed within hours, color-coded by gain or loss. You hear about movement before your competitors do.

Honest framing. NeverRanked is bootstrapped, founder-led, and currently has a small customer count. That is also the buying advantage: pilot pricing, direct founder access, and the ability to shape the roadmap with your specific use case in mind. As the platform grows, the tier you start at locks in.

05 What 90 days looks like if we work together

The 90-day window is not a sales tactic. AI models retrain on a cycle. Schema deployed in month one gets absorbed into the next training window and surfaces as citations in month three. Anyone promising AI citations in week two is selling theater.

What the engagement looks like, concretely:

  • Days 1-2. We deliver the full audit. $750, and the entire $750 is fully credited toward your first month if you decide to keep going on Pulse, Signal, or Amplify within 30 days. You get a 90-day implementation roadmap, a competitor citation matrix (which Honolulu venues currently win for the queries you care about, named, with the threads they get cited from on Reddit and the trust profiles they're using), and a schema fix list with exact JSON-LD code your dev or your CMS can paste in.
  • Days 3-30. Schema fixes ship. Event schema templated across every show. LocalBusiness/NightClub deployed. Offers attached to every ticketed event. Organization schema completed. AggregateRating wired in. Each schema runs through our 3-pass validation (factual, tone, quality) before it touches your live site, and every variant we deploy is tracked so we can prove citation lift over time.
  • Days 31-90. AI engines start absorbing the structured data. We track citation share monthly across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, Google AI Overviews, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, and Gemma on the queries that matter for ticket sales. You see who you are now appearing alongside, who is still beating you, and you get real-time citation alerts (Signal+) when a query gains or loses you in any engine. Monthly Visibility Index PDF delivered the first business day of each month.
  • Day 90 onward. Dashboard refreshes weekly. Quarterly drift detection re-evaluates the landscape so you do not go stale. The Visibility Index now includes a "what we deployed and what it did" section showing the citation lift attributable to each schema variant, with statistical confidence at p < 0.05.

Pricing after the audit: Pulse at $497/month (entry: monthly Visibility Index, 10 tracked prompts, 2 schemas/month), Signal at $2,000/month (weekly tracking on up to 50 prompts, real-time citation alerts, every schema your audit identifies, deployed), or Amplify at $4,500/month (Signal plus brand voice fingerprint, citation-shaped content drafts, auto-publish to your CMS). Three-month minimum on all monthly tiers. The $750 audit credit applies to whichever tier you pick.

06 The multi-location upside

Hawaii is the right location to start. It's a venue with measurable foot traffic from a tourism market, a clear "what's tonight" buying behavior, and a natural test case for the work. But the same gaps almost certainly exist on the New York, Tokyo, Napa, Milan, and Beijing pages, and the same fixes apply. If Hawaii moves the needle in 90 days, the case for rolling the work across the rest of the network writes itself. We can talk pricing for a multi-location bundle when there's a reason to.

07 Why us, briefly
  • We built the tools, then ran them on ourselves first. Schema grader, citation tracker, drift detection, authority audits, the public schema scorer at https://check.neverranked.com?utm_source=pitch&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=blue-note-hawaii. Same software that audited your site is what runs on our own domain.
  • The codebase is public at github.com/LanceRoylo/neverranked. You or your developer can read every line of what gets injected into your site.
  • Security details are public too. The schema injection snippet does exactly one thing — appends JSON-LD to your <head>. No cookies, no DOM read, no outbound calls. Full breakdown at neverranked.com/security.
  • Carl can vouch for me. Worth two minutes if you want a sanity check before we talk.
08 The closest analogue

Hawaii Theatre is the venue I'd point to as the closest local parallel. Same category (historic Honolulu performing arts), same starting state when they came to us: a 45 out of 100 AEO score, grade D, zero AI citations across the queries that matter for venue discovery. Ten days later, after we deployed five schema categories (PerformingArtsTheater, WebSite, AggregateRating, FAQPage, BreadcrumbList across 24 site sections, plus 35 Event schemas on a daily refresh cron), Hawaii Theatre scored a 95 and Perplexity named them on 14 of 19 tracked queries the same week. The pattern is replicable. The full case study is at neverranked.com/case-studies/hawaii-theatre.

09 Two ways to take the next step

Book the $750 audit →
Delivered in 48 hours. Includes the full citation matrix for the queries you care about (you tell me which ones, otherwise I default to live-music-Honolulu intent), the per-page schema completeness report, the competitor map (named venues, named threads), and the 90-day implementation roadmap. The audit is yours regardless of whether we work together after.

Or reply to schedule 30 minutes →
I'll walk you through the findings live, answer questions about the work, and we can decide together whether the audit is the right next move. No deck. Probably the dashboard on a screen-share.

Either way, thank you for reading this far. Tell Carl I said hi.
